notes: Persistent version control for creative + architectural intent (PRD §6 Design Memory, §12 Design Intent Repository). Every accepted or rejected decision is recorded with the five Intent Repository fields. Parse this before proposing new designs to prevent regression and repetition. In Phase 5 this file becomes the store backing the executable GDOS Design Memory; for now it is the authoritative human/agent-readable ledger.

decisions.title: TypeScript compiled by tsc, tested with node:test on compiled output
decisions.why_it_exists: The protocol schema references .ts/.d.ts and demands strict typing and data/logic decoupling; the runtime target is the web.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Provides strict static typing and a zero-extra-dependency test loop that runs on the available Node 20.10 (which cannot strip TS types natively).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (engineering-infrastructure decision).
decisions.misconception_created: None for players; for future sessions, the compiled CommonJS output is a test/runtime detail — the web build (ESM/bundled) is a separate P11 concern.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: tsx/ts-node need installs that the broken private mirror blocked; plain JS forfeits the strict typing the protocol requires; Node native type-strip requires Node 22.6+.

decisions.title: Core engine generic over an abstract world type (GameState<TWorld>)
decisions.why_it_exists: To keep src/core fully decoupled from gameplay systems and content per the directory isolation rules.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Lets the deterministic loop, clock, and RNG be built and verified in isolation with zero gameplay code present, and lets physics/gameplay plug in later without redesigning core.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: None.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A concrete monolithic GameState would couple core to gameplay and force placeholder gameplay stubs now — forbidden by the no-placeholder invariant.

decisions.title: Explicitly threaded RNG + Clock state (no hidden globals)
decisions.why_it_exists: PRD Principle 5 (Surprise Through Context, Never Randomness) and §15 (reproducible agent simulation) require the whole pipeline to be a pure function of its seed and inputs.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Guarantees bit-identical replay for the validation agents and for level generation, which are deterministic functions of a seed.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Player trust — no outcome is ever attributable to hidden randomness.
decisions.misconception_created: None intended.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Global Math.random / a singleton generator would make replays and generated levels non-reproducible and silently break the trajectory solver.

decisions.title: Immutability is the correctness contract; zero-allocation is a deferred hot-path optimization
decisions.why_it_exists: The PRD requires BOTH an immutability baseline (return modified copies) and a zero-allocation runtime loop (§17); these are in direct tension for a per-frame full-tree copy.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Resolves the tension by scoping: correctness/determinism via immutability now; zero-allocation applied in P11 to pooled hot entities (particles/projectiles) and hot inner loops, not the top-level snapshot swap.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (technical guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might read §17 and prematurely mutate the state tree in place — the handoff warns against this.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Mutating the shared tree now to chase zero-allocation would destroy determinism and the immutability invariant before any gameplay exists to justify the optimization.

decisions.title: Split GDOS delivery: governance substrate now (P0), scoring engine later (P5)
decisions.why_it_exists: PRD §9 wants GDOS early, but §6's quality gates take a level asset as input, which requires the §13 schema and §16 physics to exist first.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Honors the hard constraint (no content before GDOS/validation/tools) while scheduling the executable gates where their inputs actually exist.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: None — documented as a merge of interlocking requirements, not a reinterpretation.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Building scoring gates in P0 with no level to score would be placeholder work; deferring all GDOS would violate the design-memory-from-first-commit requirement.

decisions.title: Deterministic SDLC gate (Node + YAML recipe), default enforcement = warn
decisions.why_it_exists: Soft enforcement (CLAUDE.md, skills, reminders) relies on the model choosing to comply; mid-complex-task under context pressure it drifts (edits src without updating the PKG, marks work done without tests, skips the handoff).
decisions.problem_it_solves: Adds a hard, model-independent check (tools/sdlc/gate.js driven by tools/sdlc/sdlc.yaml) wired to a Stop hook that surfaces drift at every code turn's end, plus a manual CLI (npm run gate / gate:full).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (engineering guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: None. Note: default is 'warn' (non-blocking) because a Stop hook fires on EVERY turn incl. conversational ones and mid-task question pauses; hard-blocking those would loop and fight the workflow. Flip enforcement: block in sdlc.yaml once the gates are trusted.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Rust binary rejected for now: robust but not installed and adds a toolchain+build step for I/O-light glue. Pure YAML rejected: declarative only, cannot execute. Node chosen: zero new toolchain, cross-platform, dependency-free so it runs even if the project build is broken.

decisions.title: Engine commits a snapshot even on a zero-step tick, to persist the banked clock accumulator
decisions.why_it_exists: Clock.advance()'s accumulator is threaded through GameState.clock like tick/rng/input (see State.ts), and Engine only ever reads state via StateManager.getState() at the start of tick(). If a frame's real delta banks less than one fixed step, that leftover time must still reach the next tick() call.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Without committing on a 0-step tick, the advanced ClockState (with the updated accumulator) is computed but discarded, and the next tick() call reads the STALE pre-advance clock from StateManager — banked time is silently lost every zero-step frame and, at low frame rates, the simulation can stall permanently at effectively zero steps.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (correctness guardrail for the deterministic Read->Process->Emit loop).
decisions.misconception_created: A future reader might assume 'commit' only ever means 'a fixed step ran'; it also means 'the clock's banked time was persisted' even when zero steps ran this call.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Giving Engine its own private clock field outside GameState was rejected — it would create a second, un-threaded source of truth for clock state, violating single-source-of-truth and breaking replay (the clock would no longer be part of the (seed, inputs) => state pure function).

decisions.title: At milestone close, a REQ flips to VERIFIED only when its OWN backlog Phase-column scope is fully satisfied by the closing phase(s) — not whenever the phase's older execution-plan checklist said so
decisions.why_it_exists: execution_plan.md's original P1 checkpoint C1.5 (authored session-1, before IRD.md's cross-cutting note existed) said 'P1 REQs flipped NOT_STARTED/IN_PROGRESS -> VERIFIED' without qualification. Taken literally at M0 close, that would have flipped REQ-120/121 (backlog Phase = P1,P2 — half their scope is still unbuilt) and REQ-P08/P09 (backlog Phase = P0-P11, and IRD.md line 76 names REQ-P01-P10 as cross-cutting every phase) to VERIFIED on unproven/perpetually-open behavior.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Resolves a genuine conflict between two governance docs (execution_plan.md's simplified checklist vs. requirements_backlog.md's authoritative per-REQ Phase column + IRD.md's cross-cutting note) in favor of the more precise, per-REQ source of truth, so the four-state ledger never becomes fiction. Established the rule for all future milestone closures: read the backlog's own Phase column per REQ; flip to VERIFIED only if the closing phase(s) cover that REQ's ENTIRE Phase-column scope. At M0 this meant only REQ-P01, REQ-P05 (both backlog-scoped to P0 alone) and REQ-P10 (backlog-scoped to P1 alone) verified; REQ-120/121/160 and the seven P0-P11 protocol REQs correctly stayed IN_PROGRESS. Full rationale in docs/verification/P1.md section 4.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (governance-process guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session skimming only execution_plan.md's original (now-annotated) checkpoint line could still assume 'all P1-touched REQs verify at P1 close' — the corrected checkpoint text and this entry both flag that it's per-REQ, not per-phase-list.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Flipping every P1-touched REQ to VERIFIED per the original checklist was rejected as the anti-pattern the protocol explicitly warns against ('marking VERIFIED on unproven behavior -> the four-state ledger becomes fiction'). Leaving ALL of them IN_PROGRESS (never verifying anything at M0) was also rejected as overly conservative — REQ-P01/P05/P10 are genuinely, fully, single-phase-scoped and provably done; refusing to record that would itself be a form of ledger drift (understating real progress).

decisions.title: Static/dynamic split: tilemap is frozen static geometry; everything mutable is an entity
decisions.why_it_exists: PRD §16 says doors and collapsing floors 'modify layouts dynamically', while dm-0004's immutability contract forbids in-place mutation. If 'layout' meant the tilemap, every collapse would force a full grid copy and the loaded LevelDefinition could never be frozen or reference-shared.
decisions.problem_it_solves: The tilemap holds only permanently-static geometry and the parsed LevelDefinition is deep-frozen once and reference-shared by every snapshot (state.world.level === def forever, never copied). All dynamic elements — collapsing floors, doors, moving platforms — are entities with runtime state; 'modifying the layout' is an entity state transition. Per-frame copies touch only the small entity array, not the grid, resolving the immutability-vs-cost tension where the game is most dynamic.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architectural correctness/performance guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might expect collapsing floors to be tile edits; the schema makes that impossible by construction — they are entity kinds. Documented in docs/level_schema.md at S2.2.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Mutable tilemap layers were rejected: per-change grid copies, no freezing, and the P8 editor/P4 solver could no longer treat authored geometry as ground truth. Copy-on-write tile chunks were rejected as speculative complexity with no measured need at Jump Once's level sizes.

decisions.title: Canonical JSON serialization: finite-floats-only, -0 normalized, fixed key order, schemaVersion hard-reject, zero migration code until v2
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-122 demands lossless round-trip serialization feeding deterministic replay. Raw JSON has three determinism holes: NaN/Infinity silently become null, -0 serializes as '0' (breaking Object.is-based hashing), and key order is insertion order so equal values can serialize differently byte-wise.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Defines round-trip safety precisely and testably: the validator rejects non-finite numbers and normalizes -0 to 0 at the boundary; serializeLevel() emits a fixed field order; acceptance = deepEqual(parse(serialize(v)), v) AND serialize(parse(serialize(v))) === serialize(v). Byte-identity with the hand-authored source file is deliberately NOT required (human whitespace/key order is noise). Versioning: schemaVersion:1 required at root, loader hard-rejects any other value; migrations are pure vN->vN+1 functions written only when v2 exists — explicit accepted debt: no migration framework ships in P2.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (determinism guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might read 'lossless round-trip' as byte-identity with the authored file — it is value-level lossless plus canonical-form idempotence, per this entry.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A binary format was rejected (kills hand-authoring, editor iteration, and diffability). A schema library (zod et al.) was rejected: first runtime dependency in shipped core and a second source of truth for types; the hand-rolled validator's drift risk is priced instead by one failing-fixture test per rejection rule. Building a migration framework now was rejected as speculative debt migrating v1 to itself.

decisions.title: Data-driven values vs data-driven axioms: the single-jump rule is an engine invariant, never a schema parameter
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-120's 'strictly data-driven' read fundamentalistically would put maxJumps in the level constraints block — putting the product's identity (REQ-004/010: exactly one jump, invariant across the campaign) one typo away from falsification by data.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Draws the boundary: the constraints block carries level bounds, spawn, goal, and par-time tiers (values that vary per level); the jump count is enforced by the P3 jump-lock state machine as an engine invariant and has no schema field. Data-driven means behaviors and geometry are edited via data — not that the game's axioms are runtime-configurable.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Player trust — the one-jump promise can never be silently broken by a content file.
decisions.misconception_created: None intended; docs/level_schema.md states the axiom boundary explicitly so nobody 'helpfully' adds maxJumps later.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Parameterizing jump count 'for flexibility' was rejected: no PRD requirement wants it, every §15 validator assumes exactly one jump, and a jumps:2 level would violate REQ-010/011 by construction.

decisions.title: GDOS metadata block typed structurally-minimal in P2; semantic validation deferred to P5; P2 restructured with an instantiation slice (S2.5) as the REQ-120/121 completion evidence
decisions.why_it_exists: PRD §13 names the GDOS block's four items in one sentence; 'creator moment frame' appears exactly once in the entire PRD and difficulty-vector axes are never enumerated. Typing deep semantic structure now would be guessing on behalf of P5 (zero-assumption violation). Separately, the original S2.1-S2.5 slice plan ended at file round-trip — parsing into types nothing instantiates proves serialization, not REQ-120/121's 'runtime-parsed configuration'.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Two-layer validation seam: P2 validates structure only (targetKgNode string; difficultyVectors named-axis numeric record; emotionalBudgetCurve as keyframes of the four §6 metrics over normalized progress with strictly-increasing 'at' and [0,100] finite values; creatorMomentFrame minimal {tickWindow, description}); P5 owns semantics/thresholds. If P5 needs more, the escape path is a schemaVersion bump plus one migration function — priced and bounded. The P2 restructure adds S2.5 (WorldState + deterministic instantiateWorld in src/entities/ + engine-consumption replay test) so REQ-120/121 flip on runtime evidence, not parsing alone; verification report moved to S2.6; downstream Depends (S3.1, S8.1) updated in the same change.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (governance/scoping decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future P5 session might assume the GDOS block's shapes are semantically final — they are structurally stable but semantically provisional until P5 confirms them; open questions are logged in the P2 execution plan.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Fully specifying GDOS semantics now was rejected as invention beyond the PRD text. Omitting the block until P5 was rejected: REQ-122 explicitly requires it in the schema. Leaving P2 without an instantiation slice was rejected because P2 'completes REQ-120/121' (IRD) and would otherwise close on evidence that never runs the engine.

decisions.title: New src/schema/ directory for definition-time schema I/O; serializer/validator can never live in src/components/
decisions.why_it_exists: The first draft of the P2 plan placed serializeLevel() in src/components/LevelDefinition.ts — a function body in the logic-free directory, which S2.1's own scan test would fail. No existing directory fits schema I/O: src/components/ is logic-free data, src/systems/ is per-frame step functions (a loader is never called from the engine loop), src/core/ must stay gameplay-agnostic and generic over TWorld, and src/entities/'s charter is world construction, not payload I/O.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Gives serialize/parse/validate a sanctioned, isolated home: src/schema/ holds definition-time logic only, never imports from src/systems/, and is never called per-frame. The split is now unambiguous — schema TYPES in src/components/, schema I/O FUNCTIONS in src/schema/, world construction (instantiateWorld) in src/entities/. directory_structure.md's tree and invariants were updated in the same change.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architectural placement decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might place P8 editor export or P4 replay-tape I/O in src/schema/ by analogy — plausible and probably correct for payload formats, but each addition must be ledgered and PKG-noded like any structural change.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Stretching src/entities/'s charter to 'data initialization incl. I/O' was rejected — charter creep in directory contracts is exactly the drift the protocol warns about. Putting the loader in src/core/ was rejected: core is generic over TWorld and must not know Jump-Once-specific payload shapes. Leaving the contradiction unresolved until S2.2 was rejected: it would be discovered mid-implementation and resolved under pressure.

decisions.title: Round-trip hardening addenda to dm-0010: strict unknown-key rejection, committed golden hash of canonical form, seeded fuzz test
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0010 left three holes. (1) Unknown-key policy was unspecified: a lenient parser re-serializing a future-version file would silently drop its unknown fields — a 'lossless' round-trip that loses data. (2) 'Byte-identical across repeated calls' is trivially true for a pure function and does not catch someone CHANGING the canonical form (key reorder), which would invalidate every stored level hash downstream. (3) Enumerated rejection fixtures only cover shapes someone thought of.
decisions.problem_it_solves: (1) parseLevel strictly rejects unknown/extra keys at every path with a path-qualified error, so version drift fails loudly at parse time instead of corrupting data at re-serialize time. (2) A golden hash of the serialized sample fixture is committed; any change to the canonical form (key order, number formatting) fails a test and forces a deliberate, ledgered decision. (3) A deterministic fuzz test drives seeded mutations of the valid fixture (wrong types, nulls, truncations, injected keys) through parseLevel using src/core/Rng — every outcome must be a Result error or a valid value, never a throw, never a false accept.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (determinism/robustness guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: Strict rejection means v1 loaders refuse v1.x-authored files even when the additions look harmless — that is the intended behavior, not a bug; the escape path is a schemaVersion bump per dm-0010.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Lenient ignore-unknown-keys parsing (the common JSON default) was rejected for the silent-data-loss failure mode above. Preserving unknown keys opaquely through round-trip was rejected: it smuggles unvalidated data past the parse-don't-validate boundary and makes the canonical form input-dependent. Property-based testing libraries were rejected (dependency-free discipline); the seeded in-house fuzz over src/core/Rng is sufficient and replayable by seed.

decisions.title: S2.2 schema-shape decisions: y-down coordinate convention; tile ids {0:empty, 1:solid}; triggers as standalone wiring records; local-space waypoint polylines; four provisional difficulty axes
decisions.why_it_exists: The P2 plan deliberately deferred five concrete shape decisions to S2.2, when they must be fixed once and documented (docs/level_schema.md) because P3 physics, P4 solvers, P8 editor, and P9 renderer all inherit them.
decisions.problem_it_solves: (1) Coordinates: y-down, origin at the tilemap's top-left, gravity acts +y, tilemap row-major (tile col,row covers world [col*tileSize,(col+1)*tileSize) x [row*tileSize,(row+1)*tileSize)), all world distances in world units with tileSize the tile edge length — matches row-major authoring and canvas/WebGL screen space; only P3 physics intuition pays a small localized tax. (2) Tile ids: closed numeric set {0: empty, 1: solid} via a const record; P2 tiles are collision-relevant kinds only, visual variants are a P9 schema extension via version bump (dm-0009/dm-0010). (3) Triggers are standalone TriggerDef wiring records {id, source, targets, action, once} with their own TriggerId brand — NOT targets embedded in behavior payloads — so signal sources (plates/zones) stay decoupled from their effects (REQ-154) and the interconnection graph is first-class validatable data (REQ-122 'interconnected triggers'). Closed action union: openDoor/closeDoor/toggleDoor/collapseFloor/activatePlatform. (4) Waypoint movers: polyline of >=2 local-space Vec2 offsets from the entity transform + constant speed (world units/second) + mode linear|looping|triggered; time fields authored in seconds, converted to fixed steps deterministically. (5) Difficulty vectors: four named axes — executionPrecision, readingComplexity, timingStrictness, routeAmbiguity — each [0,1]; PROVISIONAL until P5 confirms (dm-0012's structural-now/semantic-later seam applies).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (schema-shape decisions).
decisions.misconception_created: The difficulty-axis list looks authoritative once typed — it is provisional; P5 may bump the schema version to change it. Y-down may surprise a physics-first reader; docs/level_schema.md is the single normative statement.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Y-up was rejected: it forces a flip either in tilemap row indexing (authoring pain, off-by-one magnet) or in the renderer, and the web target makes screen-space natural. Embedding trigger targets in plate/zone payloads was rejected: it couples signal source to effect and hides the interconnection graph from the validator. Per-level tile palettes were rejected for P2: two collision kinds need no indirection; a palette block is a v2 extension if P9 needs it. Parametric curves for waypoints were rejected: polylines are solver-friendly (P4 needs reachability math) and sufficient for the §16 library.

decisions.title: P3 kinematics are closed-form tick-parametric for everything non-player: derive from tick, never integrate incrementally
decisions.why_it_exists: P3 planning (adversarial review of the original slice table). Incremental per-frame integration of movers, laser cycles, and collapse timers accumulates float error over long runs, makes any-tick state reconstruction impossible, and forces reload code to re-zero a growing set of accumulators — three separate drift/bug surfaces in a project whose identity is bit-identical replay.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Moving platforms and moving hazards compute position as pathPosition(elapsedTicks) via arc-length arithmetic over the waypoint polyline (constant speed, elapsedSeconds = elapsedTicks * FIXED_STEP_SECONDS); lasers are a pure lethality function of (tick, periodSeconds, onFractionOfPeriod, phaseSeconds); collapsing floors store only firstContactTick. State stored per entity is minimal (activationTick, firstContactTick, door open flag, plate pressed flag); everything else is derived. Exactly reproducible at any tick, zero accumulation error, reload-trivial. Mover mode semantics fixed (PROVISIONAL, P5/GDOS confirm): linear = ping-pong, looping = closed-circuit wrap, triggered = dormant until trigger-activated then ping-pong. Only the player is integrated, because input makes the player's trajectory non-parametric.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (determinism architecture).
decisions.misconception_created: EntityState.velocity for movers is derived (pos(t) - pos(t-1)) / dt when needed (carry), not stored truth — a reader may expect integration where there is none. Mover mode semantics look final; they are provisional until P5.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Per-frame velocity integration for movers was rejected for the drift and reload-complexity reasons above. Storing full per-entity kinematic state was rejected: redundant state is state that can disagree with its source. Trig-parametric paths (sine bobbing etc.) were rejected: transcendental functions are banned in simulation (dm-0017) and polylines are already the schema shape (dm-0015).

decisions.title: Simulation math whitelist (no transcendental functions) + swept axis-separated collision + semi-implicit Euler
decisions.why_it_exists: P3 planning. Two silent replay-breakers exist in naive platformer physics: (1) Math.sin/cos/tan/pow/exp/hypot are NOT correctly-rounded by IEEE-754 and may differ across JS engines — a replay recorded in a browser could diverge in Node (P4 solvers) or another browser; (2) discrete AABB overlap collision tunnels through one-tile-thick geometry at spring-launch velocities, an exploit class P4 would otherwise have to filter level-by-level.
decisions.problem_it_solves: (1) src/systems/ code restricts itself to IEEE-exact operations: + - * / , Math.sqrt, comparisons, Math.min/max/abs/floor/ceil/trunc, and %. Grep-auditable at phase verification. (2) Player-vs-tilemap movement resolves per axis (X then Y) by scanning the tile range swept by the moving AABB and clamping position to the first solid tile boundary — exact snapping to col*tileSize / row*tileSize edges (float-exact values, no epsilon nudging), correct at any velocity. Player-vs-entity lethal checks use the Minkowski-sum swept segment-vs-AABB test so hazards cannot be skipped through. (3) The integrator is semi-implicit (symplectic) Euler — velocity updated first, then position — the standard stable deterministic choice under constant gravity at fixed step.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (determinism/robustness guardrail).
decisions.misconception_created: The whitelist means future mechanics needing rotation (spinning hazards) must use precomputed data or rational approximations, not Math.sin — that constraint is intended and must be surfaced at design time, not discovered as a ban violation.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Fixed-point/integer math was rejected: IEEE doubles restricted to exact ops are already cross-engine deterministic, and fixed-point would tax every downstream consumer (P4 math, P8 editor) for no additional guarantee. Continuous collision via exact time-of-impact sorting across all entities was rejected as over-engineering at this entity count: axis-separated tile sweep + Minkowski entity sweep covers the actual failure modes (tunneling through solids, skipping hazards). Velocity clamping ('never move more than a tile per step') was rejected: it silently alters authored spring behavior instead of resolving it correctly.

decisions.title: Jump-lock state lives in WorldState (reload-refresh by construction); physics/feel tuning is campaign-global pure data in src/components/Tuning.ts
decisions.why_it_exists: P3 planning. (1) REQ-010/011: the jump refreshes ONLY on scene reload. Any hand-written 'reset the lock' code path is a falsification surface for the game's axiom. (2) The data-driven invariant forbids gameplay numerals in logic, but the level schema deliberately carries no physics constants (game feel must be campaign-consistent, and dm-0011 keeps axioms out of data).
decisions.problem_it_solves: (1) All jump-lock state (machine phase, anticipation countdown) is part of WorldState; scene reload IS instantiateWorld(level) — a pure, already-proven-deterministic function that rebuilds the initial world. Refresh-on-reload therefore holds by construction: there is no reset code to forget or get wrong, and the fuzz property test (never >1 jump per life) covers the machine itself. (2) Gravity, run speed, jump velocity, anticipation ticks, player collider half-extents live in a frozen pure-data record in src/components/Tuning.ts — logic-free (the S2.1 scan test already covers the directory), readable by P5 scoring and the P8 editor, campaign-global by design. Per-level physics overrides, if GDOS ever demands them, are a schema-version bump (dm-0010 path), priced and deferred.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery through consistency: identical physics on every level makes skill transfer real (§2).
decisions.misconception_created: Tuning.ts looks like 'just constants' — it is the single normative source of game feel; editing it invalidates recorded replays and trajectory goldens, so changes are ledgered decisions, not tweaks.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Per-level physics constants in the schema were rejected: fragmenting game feel per level undermines §2 skill transfer, and no PRD requirement asks for it. A 'reset jump' method on a controller object was rejected: systems are stateless and the axiom must not depend on remembering to call a reset. Putting tuning in src/core/ was rejected: core is domain-agnostic; in src/systems/ rejected: systems are logic, tuning is data — src/components/ is the logic-free data home the scan test already guards.

decisions.title: P3 slice-table restructure: run-lifecycle slice added (S3.4), jump lock moved to S3.5, input-system ownership fixed (sim consumes state.input; live capture P9; replay tapes P4), REQ-150 visual clauses re-scoped to P9
decisions.why_it_exists: P3 planning adversarial review. The original S3.1–S3.8 table had structural gaps: no slice owned goal detection, the defeat state, or scene reload — yet REQ-010/011's acceptance ('refresh only on reload') is untestable without reload, and REQ-003's 'instant gameplay iteration' IS the defeat-reload loop. Input-system ownership was undefined (LevelInstantiation.test.ts injects InputFrames manually and says 'the input system doesn't exist until P3'). REQ-150 bundles simulation clauses (accel/decel, anticipation, lock) with render clauses (squash-and-stretch, particles, camera) but sat in P3 alone, which would force either false verification or a permanently-open REQ.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Table restructured to S3.1–S3.9: S3.4 (new) owns goal/defeat/instant-reload; the jump-lock axiom slice moves to S3.5 and depends on S3.4 (its reload-refresh test needs reload to exist); env/hazards/kinetic shift to S3.6–S3.8 with tightened dependencies (riding platforms and pressure plates need the controller, not just the physics core); verification report becomes S3.9. Input boundary decision: the simulation's input contract is GameState.input (exists since S1.4) — the P3 controller consumes it and nothing else; live device capture (keyboard/touch to InputFrame) belongs to P9 under REQ-170/171; serialized replay tapes belong to P4 (per the P2 open question, assigned at P4 planning). REQ-150's Phase column amended to P3,P9 so P3 verifies only its simulation share (dm-0008 per-REQ discipline). Save-game persistence ownership (P2 open question, must close before M1): assigned to P9 under REQ-171.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (planning integrity).
decisions.misconception_created: Slice ids S3.4–S3.8 changed meaning relative to the previous handoff's pick-list (S3.4 was the jump lock, now S3.5 is) — same renumbering pattern P2 used; task_slices.md carries the restructure note.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Folding lifecycle into the jump-lock slice was rejected: it doubles the axiom slice's surface and hides that defeat/goal/reload is REQ-003's own deliverable. A dedicated P3 input-system slice was rejected: the sim-side contract already exists in core; building device capture now would drag P9 SDK concerns into the deterministic phase. Leaving REQ-150 solely in P3 was rejected: it forces either falsely verifying unbuilt visual clauses or leaving the REQ open forever.

decisions.title: Single-jump lock semantics (S3.5): grounded-press only, air presses ignored-not-consumed, committed anticipation, forward-only machine, refresh only by re-instantiation; no coyote time, no input buffering
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-004/010/011 define THE axiom but not the frame-level semantics. Every edge case (press in air, press during anticipation, support lost mid-countdown, press after landing) needed one deterministic answer, and popular feel mechanics (coyote time, jump buffering) silently create second-jump-like behavior that could falsify the axiom's spirit.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Machine: available --(jumpPressed edge AND playerGrounded)--> anticipating(TUNING.anticipationTicks) --countdown--> ONE impulse vy=-TUNING.jumpSpeed --> spent, with NO exit from spent within a life. Air presses are ignored AND not consumed (the player keeps the jump). Presses during anticipation/spent do nothing (no restart, no stack). The press COMMITS: the impulse fires after the countdown even if support was lost (deterministic; collapsing floors cannot eat a committed jump). Horizontal control never locks — REQ-150's 'locks to horizontal-only' spends the vertical axis only. Refresh happens exclusively because reload re-instantiates WorldState (dm-0018): no reset code exists to forget. Coyote time and input buffering are deliberately ABSENT: they are GDOS feel decisions (P5) that would need explicit ledgered adoption; if adopted they must still consume the single jump. The machine lives in PlayerControl (the by-field input consumer) and its state in WorldState.jumpLock. Property test: 40 seeded 300-tick fuzz tapes across natural defeats and resets — never >1 impulse per life, never a backward phase transition within a life.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery and fairness (§2): one committed, predictable jump — no hidden grace windows to learn or exploit.
decisions.misconception_created: Players from other platformers may expect coyote/buffer forgiveness; its absence is a decision, not an omission. The committed-anticipation rule means a jump can visibly start from a surface that no longer exists — intended.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Coyote time rejected (for now): a grace window is an air jump by another name unless carefully bounded; adopting it is P5's call with the axiom property test as the gate. Input buffering rejected: buffering a press across landing approximates jump-refreshing behavior at the boundary and complicates the edge-input contract. Cancelable anticipation rejected: cancellation reopens 'available' after a press — a backward transition, which the machine forbids by design. Air-press-consumes-jump rejected: punishing a null input violates §2 logical fairness.

decisions.title: S3.8 kinetic modifiers fold into PlayerControl (spring/conveyor) and PlayerPhysics (gravity zones) rather than a separate surfaceEffects system; none may touch jumpLock
decisions.why_it_exists: The P3 plan sketched a `surfaceEffects` system slot after playerPhysics. But a post-physics velocity edit is immediately overwritten by next tick's PlayerControl instant-set (conveyor would do nothing), and gravity must be applied DURING integration (a post-physics edit integrates a tick late and mis-times the parabola). REQ-153 also demands the modifiers never consume the single jump — the axiom's most fragile boundary.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Each modifier lives where its effect integrates correctly: (1) SPRING — PlayerControl, grounded-on-spring sets playerVelocity = launchVelocity; a jump impulse the same tick still overrides vy (axiom precedence). (2) CONVEYOR — PlayerControl, grounded-on-conveyor sets vx = moveAxis*runSpeed + surfaceVelocityX (composes with walking). (3) GRAVITY ZONE — PlayerPhysics samples effective gravity = gravityY * product of gravityScales for zones whose AABB contains the player centre, BEFORE the vy update; the terminal-fall clamp bounds only downward speed so inverted gravity accelerates upward unclamped. All values are per-entity DATA (launchVelocity/surfaceVelocityX/gravityScale), not TUNING. INVARIANT: no modifier code path reads or writes world.jumpLock — a property test asserts jumpLock is bit-identical before/after 60 ticks of interaction with each modifier, and that the life retains exactly its one jump. No new WorldState fields (modifiers are stateless edits from entity data).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Kinetic momentum and surprise (§1, §16) — springs/zones/conveyors add movement variety while the one-jump identity is provably untouched.
decisions.misconception_created: 'surfaceEffects' as a named system does not exist — a reader following the P3 plan's canonical pipeline list will not find it; the effects are folded into control/physics. The spring fires on GROUNDING (not arbitrary overlap), so it models floor bounce-pads; wall-mounted sideways springs are out of scope until GDOS asks.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A dedicated post-physics surfaceEffects system was rejected: conveyor edits would be clobbered by PlayerControl's instant-set, and gravity applied post-integration mis-times the fall. Putting gravity-scale in a post-pass and re-integrating was rejected as double integration. Firing springs on any AABB overlap was rejected: the player rests FLUSH on a solid spring (flush is not overlap by the project convention), so grounding is the correct, deterministic contact signal. Adding modifier state to WorldState was rejected: the effects are pure functions of entity data + player position, so no runtime state is needed.

decisions.title: Evaluation logic lives in a new src/eval/ directory with a strictly one-way dependency: eval imports the sim, the sim NEVER imports eval
decisions.why_it_exists: P4's agents and audits fit no existing directory: they are not per-frame systems (src/systems/ runs inside the engine loop; agents drive it from OUTSIDE), not definition-time schema I/O (src/schema/), not domain-agnostic runtime (src/core/), and not data (src/components/). The dm-0013 precedent (schema/ added at P2 start) applies: when a phase introduces a genuinely new KIND of logic, it gets a directory with an explicit isolation rule, not a shoehorn.
decisions.problem_it_solves: src/eval/ holds evaluation-time logic only: agent policies, archetype parameter records, the headless harness, and (S4.2+) the audit/verdict modules. It consumes the sim strictly through public contracts (createInitialState, Engine, StateManager, FIXED_STEP_SECONDS, read-only GameState). The dependency is ONE-WAY and scan-enforced (test/unit/EvalIsolation.test.ts): nothing under src/{core,systems,components,entities,schema} may import from src/eval/ — the simulation must never know it is being judged, so evaluation can be rewritten wholesale without touching replay-critical code. The dm-0017 math whitelist extends to src/eval/ (also scan-enforced) because agent decisions feed replay tapes that must reproduce cross-engine.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust (Section 15) — verdicts about levels are credible precisely because the judge cannot influence the game it judges.
decisions.misconception_created: directory_structure.md's test/integration/ label 'solvability agent simulations' refers to TESTS OF the evaluation framework; the framework itself is production code in src/eval/, consumed later by P8 tools and the P10 pipeline. Archetype parameters live in src/eval/ as frozen records, NOT in src/components/ — they are evaluation-model data, not game data, and the one-way rule makes them unreadable from systems by construction.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Placing agents in src/systems/ was rejected: they are not System<TWorld> steps and would blur the pipeline contract. Placing them under test/ was rejected: P8/P10 consume them at production time, and test code cannot be a production dependency. A tools/ home was rejected: tools/ is process machinery (SDLC gate/stage), not typed engine-consuming logic. Extending src/schema/ was rejected: schema is definition-time I/O with no engine dependency; the harness needs the full runtime.

decisions.title: Replay-tape serialization is owned by P4/S4.1: a versioned canonical-JSON ReplayTape {schemaVersion, levelId, seed, frames[]} in src/schema/TapeIO.ts
decisions.why_it_exists: Unowned since P2 (open question #5, carried through P3): S2.5's integration test built its (seed, input-tape) in code, but P4 solvers, P8 input recording, and P9 saves all need a SERIALIZED replay format, and no REQ owned defining it. The P4 harness is the first producer of tapes, so P4 entry is where the format is fixed — before three consumers invent three formats.
decisions.problem_it_solves: One tape = one full reproduction recipe: {schemaVersion: 1, levelId, seed (normalized uint32), frames: one InputFrame {moveAxis, jumpPressed, resetPressed} per simulation tick, in tick order}. Determinism contract: same (level, seed, frames) => bit-identical final state on any JS engine. The dm-0010/dm-0014 discipline applies verbatim: fixed canonical key order, strict unknown-key rejection at every path, Result-typed path-qualified errors (never throws, including malformed JSON text), value-lossless + byte-idempotent round-trip, hard version rejection with zero migration code until v2 exists. I/O lives in src/schema/ per dm-0013 (definition-time I/O); P8/P9 adopt this format rather than redefining it.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery/trust (Sections 11, 15) — speedrun routes, validation verdicts, and bug reproductions are all durable artifacts only if a tape means exactly one run, forever.
decisions.misconception_created: The tape stores the NORMALIZED uint32 seed (what createRng actually uses), which may differ from the raw number originally passed to the harness — comparing a tape's seed to an unnormalized caller value can mismatch. frames[i] is the input that drove tick i+1 (inputs are committed before the step), not tick i. A tape does not embed the level: it references levelId; replay integrity additionally requires the same level bytes (anchored separately by the P2 golden hash).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Deferring ownership to P8 (input recording) was rejected: P4 ships tape producers/consumers months earlier, and a later format would orphan every P4 witness tape. A compact encoding (bit-packed or run-length) was rejected as premature: tapes are minutes long at 60 Hz — kilobytes — and the strict-JSON path reuses the audited P2 discipline; compression is a future schema version if ever needed. Embedding the full LevelDefinition in the tape was rejected: duplication invites drift, and the golden-hash + levelId reference already anchors level identity.

decisions.title: Agent determinism model: pure decide(state, memory) policies, archetypes as data-parameterized records over one decision core, and an agent RNG stream separate from the sim RNG
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-141's five archetypes must drive the sim such that the recorded tape ALONE reproduces the run. Two silent killers were identified adversarially at P4 planning: (1) hidden agent state (a closure counter, a cached plan) makes runs order-dependent and unreproducible; (2) an agent drawing from state.rng advances the sim stream, so a tape replay — which has no agent — leaves the RNG un-advanced and the final state diverges bit-wise, breaking the determinism contract exactly where it matters most.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Three rules, all test-enforced: (1) policies are pure — decide(state, memory) -> {input, memory}; ALL agent statefulness (held intent, replan/hesitation/pause countdowns, exploration plan, RNG cursor) threads through AgentMemory exactly like sim state threads through GameState; (2) the agent RNG is its own seeded stream, derived from the run seed by a fixed additive offset (agentStreamSeed: +2^32*phi^-1 mod 2^32, whitelist arithmetic) and threaded through memory — state.rng belongs to the simulation alone; live-final-state === replay-final-state (bit equality) is an acceptance test per archetype; (3) archetypes are FROZEN ArchetypeParams data records (replanTicks, hesitationTicks, jumpLookaheadTiles, hazardPauseTicks, cautionRadius, exploreTicks) over ONE shared sensing/decision core — behavioral distinctness is proven by pairwise-distinct tapes on a discriminating fixture, and a sixth archetype is a data change. The core senses jump availability from world.jumpLock.phase: the axiom is engine ground truth, never re-modeled (dm-0020/dm-0011).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence (Section 15) — a validation verdict is only as trustworthy as its reproducibility; bit-identical replays make every verdict auditable.
decisions.misconception_created: Archetype runs are behavioral EVIDENCE, not solvability proof — a reactive archetype failing a level proves nothing about the level (S4.2's bounded search owns classification; P4 plan finding 7). The archetypes press jump at most once per life by construction, but that is a consequence of sensing phase==='available', not a rule the engine needs them to follow — the engine's lock is the enforcement. ArchetypeParams are not gameplay tuning and are invisible to src/systems/ (dm-0022 one-way rule).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Letting agents draw from state.rng was rejected: it breaks live-vs-replay bit equality (the replay has no agent to make the same draws). Giving agents setState-style mutable classes was rejected: hidden state is unreproducible state (dm-0004 spirit). Five hand-written policy classes were rejected: five code forks drift apart and make 'archetype coverage' unmeasurable; parameters over one core keep differences inspectable data. Search-based planning inside S4.1 was rejected as scope creep: the harness/archetype layer ships first and S4.2 builds the solver on top of it.

decisions.title: Solvability via a bounded, deterministic macro-action reachability search over the frozen sim; the single-jump axiom is captured by the state signature, never counted
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-141's solvability audit must classify a level solvable/unsolvable under the exactly-one-jump rule. Archetype play alone cannot: a reactive agent failing a level is evidence, not proof (P4 plan finding 7). A systematic explorer is needed — but one that is deterministic, always halts, and treats the axiom as engine truth rather than re-deriving a jump counter (which would risk contradicting dm-0020 and re-introducing a maxJumps-shaped rule, dm-0011).
decisions.problem_it_solves: A breadth-first search over MACRO-ACTIONS: each edge holds one InputFrame for holdTicks steps; the action set is the fixed 6-way cross product moveAxis{-1,0,+1}×jumpPressed{false,true}, so expansion is deterministic. Dedup uses a quantized signature — player position on a sub-tile lattice (tileSize/4), integer-floored velocity, grounded flag, jumpLock.phase + ticksUntilImpulse, and each entity's persistent flags (collapsed/doorOpen/firstContact). Because jumpLock.phase is IN the signature, 'here with the jump available' and 'here with it spent' are distinct search states — the exactly-one-jump structure falls straight out of the simulation; the search never counts or caps jumps (the engine's lock does). Death and goal are terminal leaves: reload re-instantiates an identical world (dm-0018), so crossing a death cannot expand reachable ground — the live search space is one life. Halting is structural: a visited-signature set plus a node-count cap. Verdicts are honest about their bound: goal leaf => solvable (+reconstructed witness tape); emptied frontier => 'unsolvable'; hit the node cap first => 'inconclusive', never a false 'unsolvable'. Whitelist math only (Math.floor for quantization).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence (Section 15) — a level is certified completable only by an explicit witness the harness can replay, and rejected only when the frontier provably closes.
decisions.misconception_created: The search is a discretized approximation, not an omniscient oracle: 'unsolvable' means 'no goal reachable under this macro-action/quantization discretization within budget'. For the tiny audit fixtures it is exact (verified by classification tests), but a future generator must size holdTicks/maxNodes to the level or read the 'inconclusive' signal — it must never treat a truncated search as a proof of unsolvability. The macro-action granularity assumes holding an input is a sufficient action vocabulary; frame-perfect input alternations are outside the search's reach by design (they are also outside the intended difficulty envelope, S4.4).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Per-tick branching was rejected as combinatorially intractable (6^ticks). A jump counter / maxJumps guard was rejected: it duplicates and could contradict the engine's lock (dm-0020) and reintroduces the exact field dm-0011 forbids — the signature-carries-phase approach makes the axiom emergent instead. Exploring past death was rejected: reload resets to an identical spawn state, so multi-life paths add no reachable ground and only inflate the search. Archetype-only classification was rejected (finding 7): it under-approximates and yields false 'unsolvable'. Storing full frame lists per node was rejected for memory; parent pointers + per-edge macro frames reconstruct the witness on demand.

decisions.title: Softlock = a reachable state that can reach NEITHER goal NOR death; death is an escape (reload), not a softlock
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-141's softlock audit must find dead zones — states a player can enter and never leave. The subtle question is what counts as 'leave'. Reaching the goal obviously escapes. But so does DYING: defeat triggers an instant reload to a fresh life (dm-0018), returning the player to spawn with a new jump. A pit lined with spikes is frustrating but NOT a softlock — the player dies and retries. A pit with a solid floor and no hazard, walls too tall for the one jump, IS a softlock: trapped, alive, forever.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Over the exhaustive S4.2 reachability graph, two backward-reachability sweeps mark goalReachable and deathReachable nodes (reverse BFS from goal/dead terminal leaves). A trapped node is a reachable, non-terminal state in NEITHER set. detectSoftlock reports hasSoftlock, a representative trappedRegions sample, the deepestTrapped state (the bottom of the dead zone, the most useful single evidence), the trappedCount, and a witness tape that drives the player into the trap. The 'clean' verdict is trustworthy ONLY when the search exhausted its frontier (exhaustive=true); a truncated search reports exhaustive=false and the caller must treat it as inconclusive, never as proven-clean.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust / fairness (Section 15) — a one-jump puzzle that can strand a live player is the single most corrosive feel failure; the audit makes stranding states impossible to ship silently.
decisions.misconception_created: Softlock detection is independent of solvability: a level can be fully solvable AND still contain a softlock side-region (the oubliette fixture is exactly this). The two audits answer different questions and must both run. 'deathReachable = escape' assumes the death→reload loop is always desirable relief; if a future GDOS rule wants to forbid mandatory-death routes, that is a separate audit, not a redefinition of softlock.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Treating death as another failure (so 'trapped' = can't-reach-goal only) was rejected: it would flag every spike pit as a softlock and drown the real dead zones in noise. Forward simulation to a fixed horizon per state was rejected: backward reachability over the already-built graph is exact for the discretization and far cheaper. Reporting only a boolean was rejected: a generator needs the trapped region + a witness to diagnose and fix or discard the layout.

decisions.title: Exploit filtration: the challenge envelope is the corridor lethal-hazard danger zones; an exploit is a completion that avoids them all (resolves P2/P3 open question — envelope definition)
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-141's exploit filtration must catch 'boundary path-skips that bypass authored hazards'. The open question (P4 plan #4) was what formally marks the intended challenge. The schema has no authored spatial challenge region (creatorMomentFrame is a tick window, not geometry, dm-0012), so it must be derived.
decisions.problem_it_solves: The challenge envelope is derived data: the union of the level's LETHAL-hazard AABBs (COLLISION_CLASS_BY_KIND === 'lethal'), restricted to hazards on the spawn->goal corridor (AABB intersects the spawn/goal bounding box expanded by corridorMargin, default 3 — so a decorative corner hazard is not mistaken for intended challenge), each padded by envelopeMargin (default 0.6 world units) so a near-miss counts as engagement, and evaluated against LIVE entity positions each tick (movers/lasers track). An EXPLOIT is a completion whose swept player path never enters the envelope — the authored danger was fully bypassed. Detection reuses the S4.2 search with its new `forbidden` constraint set to the envelope (skip-constrained reachability): goal reachable while avoiding the envelope => exploit, with the skip tape as the witness (the cheese) and the bypassed hazard ids; every completion forced through the envelope => clean. auditExploit returns applicable=false when no corridor hazard exists (nothing to bypass).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery / fairness (Section 15) — a puzzle's authored challenge should be the actual path to the goal, not a skippable decoration; the filter surfaces layouts whose danger is cosmetic.
decisions.misconception_created: 'Exploit' here means the hazard is fully avoidable, which for a point hazard at the bottom of a jumpable pit is really 'the hazard is cosmetic' — the same signal a designer needs either way (engage it or delete it). The envelope is a proximity heuristic, not a proof of intent; a level whose ONLY route legitimately avoids a hazard (the hazard is off the true path) will read as applicable+exploit, which is the correct prompt to reconsider the placement. The single-jump axiom means point hazards are usually jumped clean over — so many spike-pit fixtures ARE exploitable by this definition; that is a true finding, not a false positive.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Extending the envelope full-height (a vertical column the player must cross) was rejected: it conflates 'jump over' with 'skip', flagging every crossing. A small symmetric proximity margin alone cannot distinguish a clean jump-over from a high boundary road for a single point hazard (they are the same maneuver) — so the definition instead asks the honest question 'did any completion avoid the danger entirely', which is well-posed and search-decidable. Requiring an authored challenge region was rejected: none exists in the schema, and inventing one now would be a speculative schema addition ahead of a consumer (dm-0012 discipline).

decisions.title: Five optimization tiers anchored to the archetype time spread (Discovery=First-Time, WR=Expert-Speedrunner, middle three interpolated); delta = Discovery−WR, reject below a minimum
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-101 names five routing tiers (Discovery, Good, Fast, Expert, World Record) and REQ-102 a delta metric that rejects flat layouts; the open question (P4 plan #1) was how the middle tiers derive from what the sim can actually measure. Only the archetypes produce times, and only two of them are true skill extremes.
decisions.problem_it_solves: computeOptimizationWindow runs all five archetypes and takes their completion times (ticks -> seconds via FIXED_STEP_SECONDS). Discovery is anchored to the First-Time archetype's clear (naive, hesitant), World Record to the Expert-Speedrunner's clear (optimal); the Curious-Explorer is EXCLUDED from anchoring because its dawdling is behavioral coverage, not a skill tier, and would inflate the window on a trivial level. The three middle tiers (Good/Fast/Expert) are linear interpolations at 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 of the [WR, Discovery] window — honest intermediate anchors, monotonic by construction, given only the two extremes are archetype-grounded. Robust fallbacks: if the First-Time archetype fails to clear, Discovery falls back to the slowest skill-archetype clear; if the Expert fails, WR falls back to the fastest clear; anchors are prevented from crossing. delta = Discovery − WR (seconds); a layout with delta < minDeltaSeconds (default 0.25) has no room for mastery to express and is REJECTED (REQ-102). The authored parTimeTiersSeconds is CROSS-CHECKED, never trusted: parPlausible=false when the designer's optimal par is faster than the sim's own World Record (an impossible target).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery / aspiration (Section 11) — the gap between a first clear and a world record is where long-term engagement lives; a level with no gap is flat and is caught before it ships.
decisions.misconception_created: The current archetypes differ mostly by hesitation, not by discovering shorter ROUTES, so the measured delta is presently dominated by reaction/commit time rather than route optimization — the metric is sound but its spread will widen once route-discovering behaviours (or the S4.2 search's optimal witness) feed the WR anchor. The five tiers are time targets, not distinct proven routes; a future generator may replace the interpolated middle tiers with real per-tier witness runs. minDeltaSeconds is eval-model data, tunable per campaign, not a gameplay constant.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Mapping each of the five archetypes 1:1 onto the five tiers was rejected: the archetypes are not a monotonic skill ladder (the explorer is slowest but least skilled), so their raw order does not match Discovery->WR. Trusting the authored parTimeTiersSeconds as the tier source was rejected: par times are a designer's intent, exactly the thing validation must check against ground truth, not adopt. Using the S4.2 search's optimal path as WR was deferred, not rejected: it is a better WR anchor than an archetype and is the natural S4.5 upgrade once tier-witness routing is needed — recorded as the evolution path.

decisions.title: Macro Curriculum Validation is a PURE pass over an ordered CurriculumLevel[] (local verdicts as data); the minimal chapter contract is defined here for P6/P10 to adopt or version
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-140 mandates TWO physically isolated validation passes; REQ-142 defines the macro pass's four criteria. The macro pass must judge a chapter's ARC without re-running simulations (that is the local pass's job) — and no chapter manifest schema exists yet (content is gated until M2), so the input shape was undefined (P4 plan open question #2).
decisions.problem_it_solves: src/eval/macro/Curriculum.ts is PURE data logic over an ordered CurriculumLevel[] — each record bundles a level's LOCAL verdicts (solvable/hasSoftlock/hasExploit from S4.2-S4.4), a difficulty proxy (S4.5 Discovery seconds), its required + newly-introduced mechanics, and a curiosity signal (GDOS emotional-budget). validateCurriculum returns the four §15 criteria as independent pure predicates — Cognitive Structural Mapping (no orphan mechanic requirement; <= maxNewConceptsPerLevel introduced at once), Cross-Chapter Degradation (no > maxSpikeRatio jump; no end-degradation beyond tolerance), Curiosity Progression (above floor; not monotonically dead), Graduation Assessment (finale requires >= minGraduationMechanics, all taught earlier, teaching nothing new) — plus a chapter-health precondition. Isolation is STRUCTURAL and import-enforced: the module imports nothing from src/{core,systems,entities,schema} or the harness/local pass; a scan test fails the build if it ever does. The CurriculumLevel type IS the seam between the two passes and the minimal chapter-manifest contract; when P6/P10 authors the real chapter schema it must adopt or version this shape.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Curiosity + mastery across a chapter (Section 15) — pacing, escalation, and a satisfying graduation are chapter-scale properties invisible to any single level's audit.
decisions.misconception_created: The macro pass does NOT verify a level plays correctly — it trusts the local verdicts fed to it. Its criteria are heuristics over authored/derived signals (difficulty proxy, curiosity), tunable per campaign, not proofs. The CurriculumLevel contract is minimal by design; it will grow when P6's macro state variables and P10's real chapter manifests land, and that growth is a versioning event, not a silent field addition (dm-0010 discipline). The thresholds (maxSpikeRatio etc.) are placeholders GDOS may retune in P5/P6.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-running simulations inside the macro pass was rejected outright: it violates REQ-140's isolation and duplicates the local pass. Waiting for P6/P10 to define the chapter schema before building the macro pass was rejected: the pass is needed to close P4/M2, and defining the minimal contract now (to be adopted/versioned later) is the same forward-reference discipline P2 used for GDOS metadata (dm-0012). Folding the four criteria into one score was rejected: a designer needs to know WHICH curriculum property failed and where, so each criterion reports its own findings.

decisions.title: Optimization World-Record anchor upgraded to the fastest KNOWN route — min(Expert-Speedrunner clear, bounded-search optimal) — taking the evolution path dm-0028 recorded
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0028 shipped the five-tier model with the World Record anchored to the Expert-Speedrunner archetype and flagged a limitation: the archetypes differ mostly by hesitation, not by discovering shorter ROUTES, so the measured delta was reaction-time-dominated and would MISS a route optimization the archetypes never take. dm-0028 explicitly recorded 'use the S4.2 search's optimal path as WR' as the deferred evolution path; this decision takes it.
decisions.problem_it_solves: computeOptimizationWindow now also runs the S4.2 bounded reachability search (stopAtGoal) whose BFS returns the shortest macro-path to the goal; the reconstructed witness tape length gives that route's exact tick count. The World Record tier = min(the best archetype clear, the search-optimal route) — the FASTEST route known by ANY means. The verdict reports worldRecordSource ('search' | 'archetype') and searchOptimalSeconds as evidence. Consequences: (1) on a level with a genuine shortcut no reactive archetype finds, WR drops to the search route and the delta finally reflects real route optimization; (2) on a level with no shortcut, the search ties the expert and WR is unchanged (correct — nothing to optimize); (3) WR is provably never slower than either anchor (a min), asserted by test. Applicability still requires at least one ARCHETYPE clear: the five tiers are HUMAN skill tiers, so a level only a bounded search can solve has no human tier spread and is out of scope for the window (a separate difficulty signal). The new dependency eval/local/optimization -> eval/local/search is recorded in the PKG (both directions, hash s4-g7d8e9).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Mastery / aspiration (Section 11) — the World Record must be the true ceiling of skill, including routes a first-timer would never imagine, or the optimization window understates a level's depth.
decisions.misconception_created: The bounded search uses coarse macro-actions (holds of holdTicks), so its route is an UPPER bound on the true optimum and on simple levels merely ties the per-tick expert — the WR does not always drop. The min() guarantees WR is never made worse; the search only ever pulls it down. The search route is not necessarily executable by a human at frame precision; it is the ceiling anchor, not a target tier the game promises.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Replacing the archetype WR entirely with the search route was rejected: the coarse search can be SLOWER than the per-tick expert on simple levels, so the min of both is strictly better than either alone. Making a search-only-solvable level 'applicable' (human tiers from a non-human route) was rejected: the tiers are human skill bands; such a level is a difficulty/solvability signal, not an optimization window. Shrinking holdTicks to make the search finer was rejected as the general fix: it explodes the branching factor; min-with-the-expert already recovers per-tick precision where a human route exists.

decisions.title: P5 scoring architecture: deterministic estimators over a single immutable EvidenceBundle, with ALL calibration (weights, coefficients, gate thresholds) in a versioned ScoringProfile data record
decisions.why_it_exists: The Section 6 gate numbers (Curiosity >=90, Clip Potential >=90, ...) have no physical measurement. A naive P5 invents formulas with hardcoded magic weights, producing pseudo-measurements the P7/P10 generators will Goodhart against, where every recalibration is a code change. The P5 adversarial plan review made grounding the central architectural decision before any code.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Every GDOS score is a pure function score(evidence, profile): the EvidenceBundle (LevelDefinition + GdosMetadata + the four P4 local verdicts + tapes) is assembled exactly ONCE per level (gates never re-run P4 audits — the handoff rule becomes structure), and the ScoringProfile is a strict-parsed, versioned data record holding every weight and threshold (zero numeric literals in gate logic, proven by a two-profile test). Estimators are honest proxies measuring DELIVERED vs the authored GdosMetadata intent (surprise ~ plan invalidation across attempts, confidence ~ early success rate, mastery ~ optimization delta, curiosity ~ explorer divergence). Recalibration = data change + ledger entry — the exact channel CDRE (dm-0033) drives. One seam serves P10: judgeLevel(evidence, profile) -> GdosReport.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (evaluation-architecture decision); indirectly protects every Section 6 emotional target by keeping their gates recalibratable.
decisions.misconception_created: The 0-100 scores LOOK like absolute measurements; they are calibrated proxies whose meaning is fixed by the profile version. Cross-version score comparisons are invalid without noting the profile version.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A generic rule-DSL/JSON-interpreted gate engine: an interpreter is accidental complexity with worse type safety; TS scorers over data profiles reach the same recalibration path at ~6 gates (matches the dm-0024 idiom: one core, data variants). Monolithic scoring module: violates system isolation and per-module testability. Per-gate sim re-runs: duplicated work and divergent evidence between gates. ML/statistical calibration: violates determinism and zero-dependency constraints.

decisions.title: P5 slice reorder: executable Design Memory moved AHEAD of Kill Switch and CDRE; gates emit pure DesignDecision records from day one; the store is pure text->records->text over design_memory_ledger.json
decisions.why_it_exists: The original slice table ran CDRE (old S5.6) and Kill Switch (old S5.7) BEFORE the Design Memory (old S5.8) — but both read and write memory (REQ-050/051: decisions originate from GDOS, parse history before proposing). Building recorders before the record store forces ad-hoc recording (drift) or deferred recording (debt).
decisions.problem_it_solves: New order: S5.6 Design Memory, S5.7 Kill Switch/First-Party/Subtractive, S5.8 CDRE. Recording is decoupled from storage: every gate/curation verdict emits DesignDecision records (pure data, typed in the S5.1 kernel), so S5.2-S5.5 record correctly before the store exists; the store (S5.6) is a strict parse + query (findPriorArt) + canonical append over the live ledger document, with fs at callers only and append taking the date as a parameter (no Date.now — determinism preserved).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: None; the ledger file's own header note (P5 makes it the executable store's backing file) is now being executed.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping the original order was rejected for the dependency inversion above. Gates doing their own fs writes was rejected: systems stay stateless and I/O-free (P2 discipline, dm-0013). A separate new store file was rejected: the ledger header already designates design_memory_ledger.json as the backing store; two stores would fork the single source of design truth.

decisions.title: CDRE scope pinned: a deterministic profile-evolution loop (mine outcomes -> propose as decision records -> accepted proposal = new ScoringProfile version) — not ML, not self-modifying code
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-052's 'self-improving loop that improves the design process itself' is P5's scope trap: read literally it invites ML or self-modifying machinery, both impossible under the determinism + zero-dependency invariants and unverifiable besides.
decisions.problem_it_solves: CDRE = mine(history: DesignDecision[], reports: GdosReport[]) -> CdreProposal[] (systematically failing metrics, dead design-space regions, threshold/evidence mismatches) plus apply(profile, acceptedProposal) -> next profile version. Every proposal carries the five Intent Repository fields; rejected proposals are recorded and never applied. Self-improvement is data evolution under version control — concrete, testable, and small.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: 'Self-improving' here means the CALIBRATION improves through a versioned feedback channel; the code does not rewrite itself and the loop cannot silently change gate behavior (an apply is an explicit, ledgered act).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Statistical learning over outcomes: nondeterministic/undependable under the invariants and there is no player data before P8 telemetry. Treating CDRE as documentation-only process guidance: fails REQ-052's 'executable' bar and leaves no mechanism for profile evolution, which dm-0031 depends on.

decisions.title: Design-space matrix axes are DERIVED from existing registries (entity/tile kinds, five tiers, five archetypes, six-phase arc, modifier kinds) — never hand-maintained lists; economy depth = distinct covered cells per mechanic
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-041 fixes the five axis NAMES (Mechanic x Environment x Emotion x Optimization Style x Player Type) but not their values. Hand-enumerated axis lists would drift from the sim the first time P3's registries gain a kind.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Mechanic axis = the P3 entity/tile kind registries; Environment = environmental-modifier kinds (ice/conveyor/gravity-zone/collapsing); Emotion = the REQ-015 six-phase arc (giving that REQ its P5 semantics); Optimization Style = the five REQ-101 tiers; Player Type = the five dm-0024 archetypes. Lockstep is test-enforced like DIFFICULTY_AXES. Coverage = cells exercised by a level set's evidence; Economy of Mechanics (REQ-042) = depth / mechanic count with depth = distinct covered cells involving the mechanic, making 'exhaust variations before adding a mechanic' a computable comparison the Kill Switch can enforce.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Indirectly all six arc phases — the Emotion axis makes arc coverage trackable per chapter.
decisions.misconception_created: Axis derivation means the matrix RESHAPES when a registry grows; coverage percentages are comparable only within one axis snapshot (the matrix records the registry sizes it was computed against).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Hand-maintained axis enums: guaranteed drift, the exact failure dm-0015's lockstep test pattern exists to prevent. Deferring the Environment axis until P9 themes exist: the mechanical environment (modifier kinds) is what affects PLAY; visual theming is presentation, not design space.

decisions.title: IDS 'screen' = a profile-defined tile window slid over level geometry; the same visibility primitive makes REQ-016's fairness rule computable (every death traces to a hazard visible before the death tick)
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-061 demands a per-SCREEN Information Density Score but P9 (renderer/camera) does not exist; and REQ-016's 'failure information always visually present' needed a computable P5 semantics or it would remain a slogan until P10.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Viewport dimensions live in the ScoringProfile (tiles per screen); the regulator slides that window across the level counting information elements (entities, hazards, active triggers, tile features) against min (boring) and max (overwhelm) thresholds. The window function doubles as the visibility primitive: a death in an evidence tape is FAIR iff its killing hazard was inside the window in the ticks before death — an invisible kill fails the REQ-016 fairness check at S5.4.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence (no unfair deaths) and Surprise-not-trolling (Section 2: subversion must be readable in hindsight, never arbitrary).
decisions.misconception_created: The eval window approximates the real P9 camera; when P9 lands, its camera must adopt (or version against) the profile's window dims, or IDS verdicts and rendered screens diverge.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Waiting for P9's camera: inverts the phase DAG and leaves two P5 REQs unimplementable. Whole-level density (no window): a level can be globally sparse yet locally overwhelming — per-screen is the requirement's point. Hardcoding window dims: calibration belongs in the profile (dm-0031).

decisions.title: P5 estimator evidence sources fixed (S5.2-S5.4 implementation): each 0-100 GDOS score is a named, monotonic proxy over specific EvidenceBundle fields; only the coefficients are calibration
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0031 fixed the ARCHITECTURE (estimators over a bundle, calibration in the profile) but deferred the exact evidence source per metric to each slice (P5 plan open question 1). S5.2-S5.4 needed those sources pinned so the estimators are honest, testable, and monotonic rather than arbitrary.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Emotional (REQ-055): Confidence = 100 - firstTime.reloads*penalty (0 if the first-timer never clears); Curiosity = gain * max(0, curiousExplorer/direct - 1) where direct is the fastest non-explorer clear; Surprise = gain * max reloads-before-an-adapted-clear across archetypes; Mastery = 100 * optimizationDelta/reference. The authored emotional-budget curve is the intent cross-check, reported as delivered-vs-intended findings (the parTimeTiers role from P4). Streamability (REQ-056): ReactionDensity = 100 * (deaths+hazards+kinetic)/(clearDuration*refRate); ClipPotential = clipSurpriseGain*Surprise + clipKineticGain*kineticCount (Surprise REUSED from the emotional gate — one proxy, no divergence); ReplayValue = replayDeltaGain*delta + replayRouteGain*distinctClearTimes; Shareability = profile-weighted mean of the three. InfoDensity (REQ-061): peak per-screen element count from the profile viewport slid over entity+goal tiles, regulated against min/max; the SAME viewport is the REQ-016 fairness test (a death is unfair iff its attributed killer was outside the viewport around the player lookbackTicks before impact; falls are fair). Killer attribution = nearest lethal-class entity within fairnessRadius, from replayObserved per-tick lethal positions. Every coefficient named here lives in ScoringProfile; the gate code holds none. Two-profile tests prove the verdict flips with the threshold while the score is unchanged.
decisions.emotion_targeted: All six arc phases indirectly — the estimators are the instruments that measure whether a level DELIVERS its authored emotional intent, which is the whole point of the §6 gates.
decisions.misconception_created: The proxies are directional, not calibrated to human-study ground truth (none exists pre-telemetry): a score of 92 means 'more of this evidence than a score of 80', not '92% of players felt it'. They are honest ordinal instruments the CDRE loop (S5.8) recalibrates as real signal arrives (P8 telemetry). Curiosity/Surprise/Mastery all read 0 when their evidence is absent (no explorer clear / no reloads / no optimization window) rather than a neutral midpoint — absence of evidence is scored as absence of the emotion, by design.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Composite single-number 'fun' scores were rejected: they hide which evidence drove the verdict and are unfalsifiable. Recomputing Surprise independently inside the streamability gate was rejected for a shared computeEmotionalScores import — two copies would silently diverge under recalibration. Attributing a killer by re-deriving laser on-fraction timing was rejected as over-engineering for the fairness proxy: nearest lethal-class entity within a radius is sufficient evidence and stays deterministic without threading beam phase into the observer.

decisions.title: src/eval/gdos/ is PURE over pre-assembled evidence: it runs no sim and no search. Anything needing Search or the engine (the assembler; the S5.5 Novelty/EmergentFun probes) lives at the TOP level of src/eval/, not under gdos/
decisions.why_it_exists: The P5 plan (finding 8 + validation criteria) left EmergentFun's use of src/eval/local/Search.ts as 'the one deliberate exception' to the gdos no-re-audit scan, with a hedge that S5.5 would either weaken the scan or site the file outside gdos/. A standing 'one exception' in an isolation invariant is a latent erosion point — the next search-using gate cites the precedent and the boundary rots. Decided now, before S5.5, so the invariant stays total.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Establishes a crisp rule with no carve-outs: gdos/ scores a level PURELY from the already-assembled EvidenceBundle — it never drives the Engine, never re-runs an archetype (AgentHarness), and never runs a reachability Search. Everything that EXECUTES the sim or a search lives at the top level of src/eval/ as a sibling of Evaluate.ts (the assembler already sets this precedent: it is the one module that both runs the P4 audits and touches gdos, and it deliberately sits outside gdos/). So the S5.5 EmergentFun kinetic-anchor probe (which needs Search) becomes e.g. src/eval/EmergentFun.ts, and Novelty (pure descriptor distance, no search) may live in gdos/ OR alongside it. A second EvalIsolation scan (dm-0037) enforces this: gdos/ may import AgentHarness/Evaluate/local/Search/core/Engine from NOWHERE. Reading the ARCHETYPES registry as frozen DATA (DesignSpace's player-type axis, dm-0034) stays allowed — that runs nothing. The no-re-auditing scan therefore never needs a search carve-out.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (evaluation-architecture invariant).
decisions.misconception_created: 'gdos is where all P5 scoring lives' is now false at the edges: search-driven discovery (EmergentFun) is P5 work that lives OUTSIDE gdos/ by design. The organizing axis is not 'is it P5?' but 'does it execute the sim/search?' — execution at top-level eval/, pure scoring in gdos/.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Weakening the scan to whitelist EmergentFun's Search import (the plan's hedge) was rejected: a whitelisted exception in an isolation rule is exactly how one-way boundaries erode over months — the precedent outlives the reviewer.
